title: O3N: Omnidirectional Open-Vocabulary Occupancy Prediction
abstract: 通过全方位感知理解并重建三维世界，是自主智能体与具身智能发展的必然趋势。然而，现有三维占据预测方法受限于有限视角输入与预定义训练分布，难以应用于需要在开放世界探索中进行全面安全场景感知的具身智能体。为此，我们提出O3N——首个纯视觉、端到端的全方位开放词汇占据预测框架。O3N通过极坐标螺旋状态空间模块，将全方位体素嵌入极坐标螺旋拓扑结构，实现360°连续空间表征与长程上下文建模。占据代价聚合模块引入统一体素空间几何与语义监督的原理性机制，确保重建几何与底层语义结构的一致性。此外，自然模态对齐模块构建了无梯度对齐路径，协调视觉特征、体素嵌入与文本语义，形成一致的“像素-体素-文本”表征三元组。在多个模型上的大量实验表明，我们的方法不仅在QuadOcc与Human360Occ基准测试中达到最先进性能，还展现出卓越的跨场景泛化能力与语义可扩展性，为通用三维世界建模开辟了新路径。源代码将在https://github.com/MengfeiD/O3N公开。

O3N: Omnidirectional Open-Vocabulary Occupancy Prediction
Mengfei Duan
Equal contribution
Hao Shi
Fei Teng
Guoqiang Zhao
Yuheng Zhang
Zhiyong Li
Correspondence: kailun.yang@hnu.edu.cn, zhiyong.li@hnu.edu.cn
Kailun Yang
Abstract
Understanding and reconstructing the 3D world through omnidirectional perception is an inevitable trend in the development of autonomous agents and embodied intelligence. However, existing 3D occupancy prediction methods are constrained by limited perspective inputs and predefined training distribution, making them difficult to apply to embodied agents that require comprehensive and safe perception of scenes in open world exploration. To address this, we present O3N, the first purely visual, end-to-end
O
mnidirectional
O
pen-vocabulary
O
ccupancy predictio
N
framework. O3N embeds omnidirectional voxels in a polar-spiral topology via the Polar-spiral Mamba (PsM) module, enabling continuous spatial representation and long-range context modeling across 360°. The Occupancy Cost Aggregation (OCA) module introduces a principled mechanism for unifying geometric and semantic supervision within the voxel space, ensuring consistency between the reconstructed geometry and the underlying semantic structure. Moreover, Natural Modality Alignment (NMA) establishes a gradient-free alignment pathway that harmonizes visual features, voxel embeddings, and text semantics, forming a consistent
“pixel-voxel-text”
representation triad. Extensive experiments on multiple models demonstrate that our method not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on QuadOcc and Human360Occ benchmarks but also exhibits remarkable cross-scene generalization and semantic scalability, paving the way toward universal 3D world modeling. The source code will be made publicly available at
O3N
.

1
Introduction
Omnidirectional images provide broader spatial coverage and richer contextual information, making them indispensable for emerging applications
[
zheng2025one_flight
]
,
e.g.
, autonomous driving and embodied robotics.
To achieve a comprehensive understanding of the surroundings, embodied agents must possess comprehensive scene coverage, accurate spatial reasoning, and generalized semantic understanding
[
wu2024embodiedocc
,
wang2025embodiedocc++
,
sun2025dynamic_embodied_occupancy
,
zhang2025roboocc
,
zhang2025occupancy_robots
]
.
Panoramic vision offers single-shot 360° coverage with semantically continuous cues
[
ai2025survey
,
zheng2025one_flight
]
to fulfill this objective.
In parallel, 3D semantic occupancy approaches
[
wang2023openoccupancy
,
tian2023occ3d
,
yang2025adaptiveocc
,
wang2024occgen
]
lift 2D visual evidence into 3D space
[
cao2022monoscene
,
huang2023tpvformer
,
ma2024cotr
,
wei2023surroundocc
,
zuo2025quadricformer
,
shi2025oneocc
]
and enable precise spatial reasoning.
Despite this, these methods assume that scene understanding is the recognition of a limited set of labels, which typically limits the models to predefined categories, causing them to struggle with complex and dynamic objects in open-world environments
[
zheng2024veon
,
jiang2024openocc
,
boeder2025langocc
]
.
Thus, open-vocabulary understanding is crucial, as it enables the identification and prediction of unknown object categories without prior knowledge
[
li2022language
,
cho2024cat_seg
,
xie2024sed
]
, facilitating flexible and scalable semantic representation.
To the best of our knowledge, as illustrated in Fig.
1
, this work investigates the omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy task for the first time, unifying omnidirectional visual perception with 3D geometric-semantic prediction under open-ended semantics, thereby providing a more comprehensive scene understanding (see Fig.
2
a).
Inevitably, the challenges posed by panoramic imaging remain significant, including severe geometric distortions
[
zhang2024behind
]
and non-uniform sampling
[
zheng2025one_flight
]
introduced by the Equirectangular Projection (ERP). These issues limit the precise perception of spatial geometry and the effective management of high-dimensional open-vocabulary semantics.
For example, due to extension distortion, and latitude distortion introduced by the ERP projection, as shown in Fig.
2
, regions more distant from the viewpoint occupy progressively smaller portions of the image. This characteristic amplifies the risk of overfitting
[
tan2023ovo
,
vobecky2023pop_3d
]
in the
“pixel-voxel-text”
triplet feature alignment strategy, which relies on partially visible semantics under uneven data distribution, thereby leading to misalignment of novel semantics in the joint embedding space.
To address these challenges, we propose O3N, the first pure visual, end-to-end omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy prediction framework. The novelty is three-fold: (1) We propose a dual-branch Polar-spiral Mamba (PsM) module to effectively capture finer-grained geometric and semantic details and alleviate learning difficulties caused by data discontinuities near the poles in a cylindrical voxel representation
[
zuo2023pointocc
,
ming2025occcylindrical
,
wu2025omniocc
]
. This design better aligns with panoramic geometry.
(2) Prior work in 2D open-vocabulary segmentation
[
cho2024cat_seg
,
xie2024sed
]
, shows that refining the matching costs between text and pixel features via cost aggregation is beneficial for semantic learning. We propose the Occupancy Cost Aggregation (OCA) to construct a voxel–text cost volume and aggregate it with spatial and class-wise reasoning, improving robustness to overfitting in the open-vocabulary space.
(3) The inherent modality gap between text, pixel, and voxel embeddings introduces noise, increases learning complexity, and impairs generalization to unseen semantics
[
tan2023ovo
,
cho2024cat_seg
,
zhao2025dpseg
]
. To effectively narrow the semantic gap across modalities, we propose a gradient-free aggregation mechanism, called Natural Modality Alignment (NMA).
Extensive experiments on the challenging QuadOcc and Human360Occ benchmarks
[
shi2025oneocc
]
demonstrate that O3N achieves state-of-the-art open-vocabulary occupancy prediction, achieving
+
2.21
{+}2.21
mIoU/
+
3.01
{+}3.01

mIoU on QuadOcc and
+
0.86
{+}0.86

1.54

on Human360Occ over the baseline.
Figure 2
:
(a) Omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy prediction aims to perform open-vocabulary 3D occupancy prediction solely based on pure omnidirectional visual perception;
(b) Regions distant from the viewpoint occupy a smaller proportion of pixels in the image due to the perspective effect and latitude distortion inherent in the equirectangular (ERP) projection; (c) Projection of voxel centers in 3D space onto an omnidirectional image. The projection points become increasingly dense with growing distance from the voxel centers to the imaging viewpoint.
At a glance, we deliver the following contributions:
•
We introduce the omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy prediction task and the first purely visual, end-to-end omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy prediction framework: O3N.
•
To fully adapt to panoramic geometry and the density distribution of visual semantic information, the Polar-spiral Mamba (PsM) module, Occupancy Cost Aggregation (OCA), and gradient-free Natural Modality Alignment (NMA) are introduced in O3N for enhanced spatial-semantic modeling.
•
Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and models demonstrate that O3N outperforms state-of-the-art open-vocabulary occupancy methods and even surpasses some fully supervised approaches, verifying its efficiency.
2
Related Work
Panoramic semantic scene understanding.
Panoramic understanding aims for comprehensive scene perception but is challenged by distortions and uneven sampling
[
zheng2025one_flight
]
in omnidirectional images.
For panoramic semantic segmentation, early methods project panoramic images into several patches for independent segmentation
[
yang2019pass
,
yang2020ds
]
.
Subsequent studies have explored Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) methods, including explicit
[
ma2021densepass
,
zheng2023both
,
zheng2024360sfuda++
]
and implicit
[
zhang2022bending
,
zhang2024behind
,
zheng2024semantics
,
zheng2023look_neighbor
]
knowledge alignment as well as pseudo-label generation
[
zhang2024goodsam
,
zhang2024goodsam++
,
zhong2025omnisam
]
, to enable more reliable panoramic segmentation under limited supervision.
In addition, several specialized architectures are proposed for panoramic segmentation
[
hu2022distortion
,
orhan2022semantic
,
li2023sgat4pass
,
xu2025mamba4pass
,
cao2024geometric
,
lan2025deformable
,
guttikonda2024single
,
yuan2023laformer
,
tan2025dasc
,
jiang2025gaussian
,
kim2022pasts
]
.
For panoramic semantic mapping, it aims to transform panoramic inputs into Bird’s-Eye-View (BEV) representations, where the inputs can be either surround-view fisheye images
[
samani2023f2bev
,
yogamani2024fisheyebevseg
,
li2025fishbev
,
liu2025articubevseg
]
or a single panoramic image
[
wenke2025dur360bev
,
wei2024onebev
,
teng2024360bev
,
zhang2025humanoidpano
]
.
Recently, research has shifted towards 3D semantic occupancy prediction, with multi-sensor, multi-view approaches achieving significant progress.
By combining multiple fisheye cameras
[
pan2024generocc
,
cui2025humanoid
]
, depth information
[
wu2025omniocc
]
, and point clouds
[
zuo2023pointocc
,
zheng2025doracamom
,
yang2025metaocc
]
, these approaches greatly enhance semantic understanding and spatial geometry modeling in dynamic environments.
Further, OneOcc
[
shi2025oneocc
]
implements purely visual panoramic semantic occupancy prediction for legged robots.
Despite significant progress in panoramic 3D semantic modeling trained on predefined categories, existing methods lack the ability to recognize unseen semantics, leading to limitations when encountering unknown scenes in the open world.
Open-vocabulary occupancy prediction.
A dominant line of work in 3D occupancy prediction follows a closed-set paradigm, where models are trained to recognize predefined semantic categories from LiDAR or multi-view inputs
[
wei2023surroundocc
,
li2023voxformer
,
cao2022monoscene
,
tong2023scene_as_occupancy
,
huang2024selfocc
,
shi2024occupancy_set_points
,
zhu2024nucraft
]
.
These approaches focus on efficient spatial representation
[
zhang2023occformer
,
huang2023tpvformer
,
tang2024sparseocc
,
huang2024gaussianformer
,
ma2024cotr
]
, contextual reasoning and semantic generalization
[
oh2025_3d_prototype
,
duan2025sdgocc
,
zhang2025occloff
]
, yet remain limited to fixed vocabularies. Open-vocabulary occupancy prediction methods enable models to predict the occupancy of new, unseen object categories from natural language input without explicit labels. OVO
[
tan2023ovo
]
pioneered a framework for open-vocabulary occupancy by extracting knowledge from a frozen 2D open-vocabulary segmenter and CLIP
[
radford2021learning
]
text encoder into a 3D model.
Subsequent research primarily focuses on multimodal fusion
[
jiang2024openocc
]
, self-supervised learning
[
vobecky2023pop_3d
,
gao2025loc
]
, foundation model adaptation
[
zheng2024veon
,
zhou2025autoocc
]
, neural radiance fields ensembles
[
boeder2025langocc
]
, progressive gaussian modeling
[
li2025pgocc
,
zhou2026LegoOcc
]
, and pseudo-labeled reconstruction
[
yu2024language
,
li2025ago
]
to reinforce open-vocabulary perception.
Unlike existing methods that rely on fixed input perspectives and predefined training categories, our method leverages flexible omnidirectional vision and open-vocabulary understanding to break such rigid limitations, enabling more robust generalizable scene comprehension beyond traditional closed-world assumptions.
Figure 3
:
O3N architecture.
O3N takes equirectangular omnidirectional image as input and is fully end-to-end trained. The image features and text embeddings are pre-extracted via the language-image encoder. The 3D decoder, enhanced with the proposed Polar-spiral Mamba (PsM) module, captures both geometric and semantic dependencies across directions. The Occupancy Cost Aggregation (OCA) and Natural Modality Alignment (NMA) modules integrate pixel, voxel, and text modalities to achieve consistent open-vocabulary semantic reasoning in 3D space. We efficiently train the O3N through semantic occupancy of visible classes, occupancy cost aggregation, and voxel-pixel consistency during training.
3
Methodology
An overview of the proposed O3N framework is depicted in Fig.
3
.
In this work, we focus on vision-based occupancy prediction.
In contrast to existing studies, the proposed method leverages omnidirectional images as visual input, ensuring the integrity and continuity of both spatial geometry and semantic information.
To more comprehensively advance the understanding of open world, unlike other multi-modal and -stage methods
[
vobecky2023pop_3d
,
jiang2024openocc
,
zheng2024veon
,
yu2024language
,
gao2025loc
]
, we introduce the first purely visual, end-to-end omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy prediction framework, providing a new solution for comprehensive scene understanding.
Specifically, O3N includes the following components: (1) a visual feature extractor for omnidirectional images; (2) a 2D-to-3D view transformation that generates both 3D cube and cylindrical voxels; (3) a 3D decoder capable of learning fine-grained spatial geometry and semantic information; and (4) an occupancy prediction head. Through the close integration of these modules, O3N can not only perform closed-set semantic occupancy prediction but also demonstrate strong transferability.
Furthermore, the proposed Polar-spiral Mamba (PsM, see Sec.
3.1
) module enhances omnidirectional spatial perception capabilities effectively.
For 3D open-vocabulary perception, the Occupancy Cost Aggregation (OCA, Sec.
3.2
) and a gradient-free Natural Modality Alignment (NMA, Sec.
3.3
) technique are proposed to enforce the
“pixel-voxel-text”
semantic consistency, and effectively mitigate overfitting.
This unified design not only enhances perception completeness but also endows 3D occupancy prediction with semantic plasticity: a key step toward scalable world modeling.
3.1
Polar-spiral Mamba Module
Due to the limitations of angular partitioning, cylindrical voxels exhibit inherent discontinuity in data format, leading to a disruption in spatial continuity near the poles, which contradicts the original intent (
i.e
.
, spatial perception originates from the center, with higher precision in closer regions). Standard 3D convolution struggles to adapt to this critical characteristic, and the high computational cost of transformers is also prohibitive for real-world deployment.
To preserve the continuity of angular space effectively, as shown in Fig.
4
, we propose the Polar-spiral Mamba (PsM) module with a dual-branch framework to better utilize the intrinsic spatial structure of omnidirectional images.
We utilizes Spatial-Mamba
[
xiao2024spatial
]
to capture the intricate structures within the polar coordinate system, which offers transformer-like long sequence modeling capabilities with linear complexity.

3.3
Natural Modality Alignment
Although CLIP exhibits strong generalization ability, it struggles with the inherent domain gap between image and text embeddings, even after extensive alignment during training
[
zhao2025dpseg
,
tan2023ovo
]
.
This issue is further exacerbated by the projection errors inherent to panoramic images. Under the pixel-voxel feature alignment, the
“pixel-voxel-text”
semantic consistency cannot be effectively guaranteed, thereby struggling to recognize novel semantics.
Moreover, since only base class semantics are available during training, directly employing a learning-based strategy will inevitably develop excessive reliance on the distribution of seen semantics while deteriorating the ability to understand unlimited semantics.
To overcome these issues, we propose a gradient-free natural modality gap alignment strategy between text embeddings and semantic prototypes prior to occupancy cost aggregation. Specifically, it iteratively aggregates text embeddings with prototypes of seen semantics, which are obtained by filtering the base class pixel embeddings with the Exponential Moving Average (EMA) method
[
cai2021exponential
]
during the training phase, achieving co-optimization in shared embedding space until convergence.

Conclusion
In this paper, we introduce the omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy prediction task for the first time and propose O3N, the first purely visual, end-to-end framework to unify omnidirectional visual perception, spatial geometry, and open-vocabulary semantic prediction.
It enables seamless continuous spatial perception and long-range context modeling across 360° via the proposed Polar-spiral Mamba (PsM) module.
Furthermore, the Occupancy Cost prediction (OCA) offers a unified geometric and semantic learning mechanism, ensuring that the reconstructed geometry closely aligns with the underlying semantics.
The Natural Modality Alignment (NMA) synchronizes visual features, voxel embeddings, and textual semantics, resulting in a consistent, robust
“pixel-voxel-text”
triplet representation.
O3N achieves state-of-the-art open-vocabulary occupancy prediction under omnidirectional visual perception, offering a new perspective for comprehensive scene understanding in open-world scenarios.

Appendix 0.A
Additional Methodological Details
0.A.1
Unified and cohesive solution.
O3N builds upon the existing occupancy architecture to facilitate 3D understanding from a single input. As in Fig.
S1
, O3N introduces three intertwined designs, PsM, OCA, and NMA, that jointly address the unique challenges:
geometric distortion
,
semantic generalization
, and
cross-modal alignment
.
Regular Cartesian grids misalign with the 360° field of view without PsM, causing spatial misregistration that corrupts both geometry and semantics; OCA builds upon PsM to ensure geometric-semantic consistency in voxel space, enabling semantic propagation across entire 3D regions, whereas existing methods rely on discrete closed-set supervision; finally, integrating NMA closes the loop, which constructs a robust
“pixel-voxel-text”
representational triplet.
Without the alignment, backpropagating through the text encoder forces the model to overfit to training semantics, impairing generalization to unseen concepts.

:
Comparison of the existing semantic occupancy (a), open-vocabulary occupancy (b) paradigms and ours (c).
0.A.2
Cubic
vs.
Cylindrical Voxel Projections
We visualize how different voxel parameterizations project onto an equirectangular omnidirectional image in QuadOcc.
In Fig.
S2
, the points indicate the projected voxel centers, and the color bars show the normalized depth distribution of occupied voxels along each azimuth ray. Traditional axis-aligned Cartesian cubic voxels, when projected onto a panoramic image in the world coordinate system, form fan-shaped regions: voxels at different heights yield similar depth patterns along inclined rays, and distant structures, such as roads, terrain, and building facades, are compressed into narrow bands near the equatorial zone. While this configuration enables uniform metric sampling in 3D space, it disrupts the angular regularity inherent to panoramic imagery. In contrast, camera-centric omnidirectional cylindrical voxels employ equal step sizes in both azimuth and height, producing nearly horizontal sampling rows whose projections follow the equidistant cylindrical-projection parameterization and maintain the circular continuity of distant structures. This complementarity motivates combining the two voxel meshes and applying the PsM module to jointly preserve near-field metric fidelity and far-field azimuthal coherence.
Figure S2

vs.
cylindrical voxel projections on an omnidirectional image.
The projection of Cartesian cubic voxels in the omnidirectional image reflects the sparsity and imbalance of projection points.

Subsequent to spatial aggregation, a linear transformer
[
katharopoulos2020transformers
]

Appendix 0.D
Discussions
0.D.1
Limitations and Future Work
The performance of omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy prediction is dependent on the vision-language models, but a vision-language model tailored for omnidirectional vision is scarce in the literature.
The extracted open-vocabulary 2D image pixel features are prone to semantic shifts, limited generalization, and distribution bias in valid voxel sampling.
Future research could explore self-supervised learning methods for omnidirectional visual scenes to enhance the generalizability in semantic understanding.
Despite these limitations, O3N advances comprehensive scene understanding by unifying omnidirectional visual perception with 3D geometric-semantic prediction under open-ended semantics within a purely visual, end-to-end framework.
0.D.2
Failure Case Analysis
Fig.
S7
presents two typical failure cases of O3N.
Depth ambiguity is an inherent challenge in monocular 3D reconstruction. While depth-based Lift-Splat-Shoot (LSS) projection
[
philion2020lift
]
is common in pinhole vision, panoramic depth estimation contains substantial noise in the predicted depth maps, which severely compromises spatial perception accuracy. Given these challenges, we adopt the Features Line of Sight 2D-to-3D Projection (FLoSP) projection strategy
[
cao2022monoscene
,
mei2024sgn
]
, verified in OneOcc
[
shi2025oneocc
]
, as it is more concise and robust than learnable LSS
[
philion2020lift
]
and cross-attention mechanism
[
li2024bevformer
]
, and optimal for panoramic settings.
However, the FLoSP method is insensitive to overall spatial depth, so in the first case, nearly all methods using FLoSP exhibit misalignment in spatial range perception. Specifically, when the sidewalk is adjacent to vegetation, all methods demonstrate additional prediction errors. Furthermore, due to the visual similarity between the pole and sidewalk, both MonoScene and OVO fail to detect the pole, while O3N accurately reconstructs the occupancy state of the pole. This demonstrates that O3N can effectively perceive both geometric and semantic information in space, thereby showcasing enhanced generalization ability.
Figure S7
:
Failure case analysis on H3O Homo (with-city).
Similar to MonoScene
[
tan2023ovo
]
, O3N employs the same 2D-to-3D projection strategy (FLoSP), resulting in limited sensitivity to spatial depth. This limitation causes inaccuracies in accurately perceiving the full spatial extent of the scene.
In the second case, in a rainy street scene at dusk, due to the dim lighting and interference from rain-reflected light, MonoScene misidentifies most of the sidewalk as unoccupied (
empty
). OVO and O3N also encounter similar issues, but, compared to OVO, O3N more robustly reconstructs the occupancy state of the entire scene and demonstrates clearer semantic awareness. For example, OVO reconstructs the sidewalk as a building, while O3N reconstructs it as road, which is more semantically similar to the sidewalk.
These failures demonstrate that while current 2D-to-3D projection strategies can effectively extend omnidirectional images into 3D space, they still have inherent limitations in spatial localization and are prone to structural misjudgments. Therefore, enhancing the spatial depth perception of models to mitigate projection-induced geometric biases constitutes an important direction for future research. Furthermore, to address complex environmental factors, such as insufficient lighting and reflections caused by rain or snow, large-scale data augmentation and domain adaptation across different weather conditions and scenes can improve the robustness and generalization performance, especially in extreme weather and previously unseen scenarios.
0.D.3
Societal Impacts
Omnidirectional open-vocabulary occupancy prediction has the potential to significantly enhance the safety of autonomous driving systems and the perceptual intelligence of smart devices, while also accelerating the adoption of embodied AI technologies in healthcare, manufacturing, and service robotics.
However, the method remains imperfect in prediction accuracy, and in practical applications, these limitations and the associated uncertainties must be carefully considered.
preview_text:
Understanding and reconstructing the 3D world through omnidirectional perception is an inevitable trend in the development of autonomous agents and embodied intelligence. However, existing 3D occupancy prediction methods are constrained by limited perspective inputs and predefined training distribution, making them difficult to apply to embodied agents that require comprehensive and safe perception of scenes in open world exploration. To address this, we present O3N, the first purely visual, end-to-end Omnidirectional Open-vocabulary Occupancy predictioN framework. O3N embeds omnidirectional voxels in a polar-spiral topology via the Polar-spiral Mamba (PsM) module, enabling continuous spatial representation and long-range context modeling across 360°. The Occupancy Cost Aggregation (OCA) module introduces a principled mechanism for unifying geometric and semantic supervision within the voxel space, ensuring consistency between the reconstructed geometry and the underlying semantic structure. Moreover, Natural Modality Alignment (NMA) establishes a gradient-free alignment pathway that harmonizes visual features, voxel embeddings, and text semantics, forming a consistent "pixel-voxel-text" representation triad. Extensive experiments on multiple models demonstrate that our method not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on QuadOcc and Human360Occ benchmarks but also exhibits remarkable cross-scene generalization and semantic scalability, paving the way toward universal 3D world modeling. The source code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/MengfeiD/O3N.
